questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education does a broker applicant need?
questions.explanation: A Mississippi broker applicant must complete 120 hours of approved pre-license education and have at least 12 months of active experience as a licensed salesperson. An applicant who has not held an active salesperson license for at least 36 months must instead complete 150 college-credit classroom hours (§73-35-7).

questions.question_text: A salesperson wants to upgrade to a broker license. How much active salesperson experience is required?
questions.explanation: Mississippi requires a broker applicant to have at least 12 months of active experience as a licensed salesperson before applying for a broker license, in addition to completing 120 hours of pre-license education.

questions.question_text: A new licensee completes 20 hours of post-license education in her first year. What happens to her license?
questions.explanation: New licensees hold a temporary license and must complete the 30-hour post-license course within 12 months of issuance. Completing only 20 hours means the temporary license is automatically placed on inactive status by MREC; if the course is not finished and the permanent license issued within the license year, the temporary license expires and lapses (§73-35-14.5).

questions.question_text: How are the 30 required post-license hours divided?
questions.explanation: Mississippi requires 30 hours of post-license education within the first 12 months: a minimum of 24 hours in commission-prescribed core subjects (agency relationships, contracts, earnest money, antitrust, fair housing, ethics, property condition disclosure) and 6 hours of electives that enhance competency. Failure to complete on time results in the temporary license being placed on inactive status (§73-35-14.5).

questions.question_text: A new salesperson obtained her license in January. By when must she finish post-license education?
questions.explanation: Post-license education must be completed within the first 12 months of the temporary license's issuance. For a January licensee, this means by the following January. Failure results in the temporary license being placed on inactive status, and it lapses if the license year passes without completion (§73-35-14.5).

questions.question_text: How many total hours of continuing education must a licensee complete each biennium?
questions.explanation: Mississippi requires 16 clock hours of approved continuing education per two-year renewal period (§73-35-18). MREC designates the mandatory subjects — Mississippi license law, contract law, and agency — which may not exceed 8 of the 16 hours; the remaining hours are electives.

questions.question_text: Of the 16 hours of continuing education required each renewal period, what is the maximum number MREC may designate as required subjects?
questions.explanation: Miss. Code §73-35-18(1) requires 16 clock hours of approved CE per two-year renewal and lets MREC determine the required subject matter, 'provided that the required subjects shall not exceed eight (8) hours of the total sixteen (16) hours.' MREC's designated mandatory topics are Mississippi license law, contract law, and agency (Rule 6.2.D); the remaining hours are electives.

questions.question_text: A 67-year-old broker has held a Mississippi license for 30 years. Is she exempt from continuing education?
distractors: No, all licensees must complete CE
Only if she is inactive status
Only if MREC approves a waiver
questions.explanation: Mississippi exempts from CE those licensees who are older than 65 AND have held a Mississippi broker's or salesperson's license for at least 25 years (§73-35-18). At 67 with 30 years licensed, she meets both criteria.

questions.question_text: A 66-year-old licensee has been licensed for 20 years. Does she qualify for the senior CE exemption?
questions.explanation: The senior CE exemption requires BOTH being older than 65 AND at least 25 years holding a Mississippi license. At 66 she satisfies the age test, but with only 20 years licensed she falls short of the 25-year requirement (§73-35-18).

questions.question_text: How many excess CE hours earned in the final three months of a renewal period may be carried over to the next renewal period?
questions.explanation: Approved CE hours earned in the final three months of a licensee's renewal period, if in excess of the required 16 hours, may be carried over and credited to the next renewal period — but no more than 6 hours may be carried over this way (§73-35-18(1)).

questions.question_text: A licensee places her license on inactive status. Can she continue to practice real estate?
distractors: Yes, but for existing clients only
questions.explanation: A licensee with an inactive license cannot engage in any real estate activities that require a license. The license must be reactivated before the licensee can resume practicing.

questions.question_text: SB 2086 (2025), which preserved MREC's license fee provisions by deleting their repealer, took effect on what date?
questions.explanation: SB 2086 (2025) amended Section 73-35-17 to delete the scheduled repealer on MREC's application and licensure fee provisions, keeping the existing fee authority in place. It became law effective July 1, 2025; it did not change any fee amounts.

questions.question_text: Under a 2025 Mississippi law, how much notice must MREC give before suspending a license?
questions.explanation: Under Miss. Code §73-35-10(3) (SB 2423, effective July 1, 2025), MREC must deliver written notice by email at least 5 business days before the effective date of any license change — amendment, suspension, revocation, or non-renewal. The notice goes to the email addresses on file for the licensee and the responsible broker.

questions.question_text: A licensee's renewal deadline passes without renewal. What is the license status?
questions.correct_answer: Automatically cancelled after notice and grace period
questions.explanation: Under Section 73-35-17, a licensee who fails to pay the renewal fee is sent written notice of delinquency; a licensee renewing during the two-month grace period pays a 100% penalty, and after the notice period the license is automatically cancelled. The delinquency warning is now delivered by email to the licensee and responsible broker rather than certified mail (SB 2748, effective July 1, 2026). The licensee cannot practice until the license is restored through MREC's procedures.

questions.question_text: MREC is about to make a licensee's revocation effective. Under the 2025 law, what notice must MREC give before the effective date?
distractors: Publish the action in a local newspaper
questions.explanation: Under Section 73-35-10(3) (effective July 1, 2025), MREC must deliver written notice by email to the licensee and, if applicable, the responsible broker at least 5 business days before the effective date of any license change — amendment, suspension, revocation, or non-renewal. This notice requirement is in addition to the disciplinary hearing process.
